title: Lesson 431
transcript: Congratulations, you have made it through all the fundamentals of cryptography. We've talked about how you can achieve all five services of cryptography, confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin and delivery and access control. We've talked about symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, digital certificates, digital signatures, hashing, all of this. congratulations you've made it through all of that and what i'm now going to show you is one really cool slide that puts all of that together this one really cool slide shows us where do you use digital certificates versus digital signatures why do we sometimes use symmetric photography versus asymmetric where do we use hashing and why how and where do we achieve all five services so this one slide is going to put all of that together. It's a really cool slide. I love it. If you're watching this for the very first time, I have a really important suggestion, okay? Don't copy anything down. Just look up to what I'm saying here, pay attention, don't copy anything down. I'll tell you why at the end. Okay, so let's get into it. The one slide that puts everything together. Okay, so here's what's going to happen, okay? Alice wants to send a very large file to Bob. And in sending that very large file to Bob, Alice wants to achieve all five services of cryptography. Confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin delivery and access control so let's see how she does that let's look at all the steps that Alice has to go through and Bob have to go through to achieve this now Before Alice can start sending anything, the large file to Bob, the first thing that Alice and Bob need a copy of is they need a copy of each other's public keys. But remember, you should never, ever, ever just send your public key to someone because somebody could intercept it and replace it with their own and you wouldn't know. So instead, what Alice and Bob are gonna send to each other is their digital certificates, right? Because that's the main purpose of digital certificates. It's a way of securely getting your public key to someone and for them to be able to verify they've received your public key. So Alice and Bob are gonna send each other their digital certificates. Now, let's start with Alice. When Alice gets Bob's certificate, the first thing she needs to do is she needs to decrypt it. Because this digital certificate is encrypted, and it was encrypted with the CA's private key. So therefore, it's going to decrypt with the CA's public key, which Alice has built into her system. Now, when Alice decrypts Bob's certificate, she's going to see that it says, aha, this certificate belongs to Bob and contained within it is going to be a copy of Bob's public key. So the whole purpose of this is this has allowed Alice to get a copy of Bob's public key and verify that she got Bob's public key and not some sketchy other person's public key. And Bob, of course, has done the same thing over here. He's decrypted Alice's digital certificate, confirmed that it said it belongs to Alice, and now Bob has verified that he has a copy of Alice's public key. And this is super important. Alice and Bob need to know with certainty that they have a copy of each other's public keys. I'll show you why in a minute. Here is the very large file that Owls wants to send over to Bob. And in sending this file, Alice wants to achieve confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, etc. So how is she going to do that? Let's start with the confidentiality. How is she going to achieve confidentiality? How is she going to make sure that no one can read this file in transit? Well, she's going to have to encrypt it. Now, because this is a large file, what is the only type of cryptography that's fast enough to encrypt this large file quickly and efficiently? Symmetric cryptography. So Alice is going to have to encrypt with a symmetric algorithm here, like say AES. And if Alice is encrypting with a symmetric algorithm, she's going to need to use a symmetric key. But now Alice has a problem, right? It's the Achilles heel of symmetric cryptography, symmetric key distribution. How is she gonna get this symmetric key over to Bob in such a way that no one else can intercept that key and get a copy of it? She could of course use out-of-band key distribution. She could like carrier pigeon it to Bob or something, but that's not very efficient. Here's a way that Alice can securely get the symmetric key to Bob, how she can solve the symmetric key distribution. And it's using, of course, asymmetric cryptography. That's what we use asymmetric cryptography for all the time to solve the symmetric key distribution problem. So here's what Alice is going to do. She's going to encrypt this symmetric key in such a way that the only person in the world that can decrypt it is Bob. So how does she do that? Well, she encrypts with Bob's public key. So here, right here, this is why Alice needed to know that she had a copy of Bob's public key. Cause she's going to encrypt this symmetric key using Bob's public key as the encryption key. And that's going to generate a little bit of ciphertext. And just a second here, like, Why could Alice use asymmetric cryptography here where here she had to use symmetric? And the answer is right here, right? She was encrypting a very large file. So she needed something that was fast and efficient. Here, she's only encrypting a Symmetra key. Symmetra keys are minuscule, right? A Symmetra key is like 128 bits, maybe 256 bits. A Symmetra key is a few hundred bits. And with modern computer systems, a few hundred bits is a completely inconsequential amount of data. It's tiny. And so even though asymmetric cryptography is really inefficient, it doesn't matter because all we're doing is encrypting a tiny little symmetric key. So that's why Alice can use asymmetric cryptography here. Okay, so she's encrypted with Bob's public key, and she now sends that ciphertext across the internet. Anyone can intercept this ciphertext, but the only person in the world that's gonna be able to decrypt it is Bob with his private key. And so now Bob has a copy of the symmetric key. And the whole point of this swim lane here was to solve the symmetric key distribution problem, to get a copy of this symmetric key over to Bob securely. Okay, now before Alice sends the ciphertext of the file over to Bob, there's one more thing that she wants to do. In sending this file, she also wants to achieve integrity, authenticity, and non-reputation. As soon as you hear those things, right, integrity, authenticity, non-reputation, you should think digital signatures. So the next thing Alice is going to have to do is she's going to have to create a digital signature for this file. Recall, there's really only two easy steps required to create a digital signature. Step one, hash the data. So Alice is going to run the data through a hashing algorithm, which is going to produce a fixed length message digest, a hash value. And then the second step required to create a digital signature is the sender encrypts the hash value with the sender's which key? Private key. So Alice is going to encrypt this hash value with her private key. And that's going to create a digital signature. So there's now two separate pieces of ciphertext that Alice is sending over to Bob. The first piece of ciphertext is the giant encrypted file. And the second piece of ciphertext is this tiny little digital signature here. It's just a tiny little bit of ciphertext that Alice could append to the file. So Alice sends these two pieces of ciphertext over to Bob. Now Bob decides that the first thing that he wants to decrypt is the actual file. So now the question is, what key does Bob need to use to decrypt this file? Well, remember, this file, because it's a very large file, right? The only type of cryptography that was fast enough to perform the encryption was a symmetric algorithm. with this Symmetra key. So what key is gonna be used to decrypt? The same Symmetra key, right? This is why we needed to solve, this is why Alice needed to solve the Symmetra key distribution problem. In order to decrypt the large file, Bob's gonna need this Symmetra key. Okay, so Bob decrypts the file. Now, a really good question to contemplate at this point is right here where Bob has decrypted the file, which of the five services have been achieved at this point? Remember the five services are confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation and access control. So which of the five services have been achieved at this point? There's only two. Which two? The answers are, first of all, confidentiality, right? We've achieved confidentiality because the file was sent encrypted. I know some of you are thinking integrity, authenticity, or non-repudiation. Wrong! Those only come with the digital signature. So it's not integrity, it's not authenticity, it's not non-repudiation. Those all come from the digital signature. So the only one that's left is access control. And there is actually a form of access control here, right? Because Alice is able to control who she sends the ciphertext to and who she sends the symmetric key to. So because Alice has control over who she sends those two pieces to, she has a degree of control over who can decrypt the file. That's a simple form of access control. So at this stage right here, where Bob has decrypted the file, the only two services that have been achieved are confidentiality and access control. Next thing. Right. And now let's move on to the integrity, the authenticity and non-repudiation. Those come from the digital signature. So the next thing that Bob has to do is Bob has to decrypt this digital signature. With what key? Well, this digital signature was encrypted with Alice's private key. Therefore, it will decrypt with Alice's public key. So Bob is going to decrypt Alice's digital signature with Alice's public key. Now, this is really important. Pay attention here. The fact that Alice's digital signature decrypts with Alice's public key means that the only person in the world that could have created this digital signature is Alice. And that proves authenticity. This digital signature has to have come from Alice, right? Because she's the only one that could have created it with her private key. If it decrypted with her public key, it had to have been created with her private key. So right here, just the fact that this digital signature decrypts with Alice's public key, that proves authenticity. but we don't yet have integrity and non-repudiation. Those come in a minute here. Okay, so Bob decrypts Alice's digital signature with Alice's public key, proving authenticity. Contained within Alice's digital signature is the hash value that Alice generated. These are the same hash value. Now this hash value by itself proves nothing. So the next thing that Bob has to do is Bob has to hash the file that he received, generating his own new hash value. He'll now compare the hash value that he just generated to the hash value that he got from Alice's digital signature. And if these hash values match, that proves integrity. That's what hashing proves, integrity. So if these hash values match, it means that Bob has received exactly the same file that Alice sent. And now that we have authenticity and integrity, this is where we now achieve non-repudiation of origin. Alice cannot deny she sent this file because Bob can say, look, I know this digital signature came from you, authenticity. And I know this is exactly the same file that you sent. Integrity. So when you have authenticity and integrity, that's where you get non-repudiation. You can only have non-repudiation if you have both of those. Integrity and authenticity. Okay. Now there's only one final piece missing here, and that's non-repetition of delivery. Alice wants to know without a doubt that Bob actually got this file. So how do we achieve non-repudiation of delivery? Well, whenever you hear non-repudiation, you should think digital signatures. So what's Bob gonna have to do? He's gonna have to send his own digital signature back to Alice. Remember, there's just two easy steps required to create a digital signature. Step one, hash the data. Bob's already done that, right? He's got the hash value right here. So all Bob has to do is take this hash value. And then step two of creating a digital signature is the sender encrypts with the senders. private key so bob will encrypt that hash value with his private key generating his own digital signature which he's going to send back to alice And now Alice will go to decrypt Bob's digital signature using Bob's public key. And if this digital signature decrypts with Bob's public key, who is the only person in the world that could have created the digital signature? Bob with his private key. So that proves authenticity. And then contained of course, within Bob's digital signature is the hash value that Bob created over here. And the final thing that Alice is gonna do is she's gonna compare the hash value that she generated with the hash value that she got from Bob's digital signature. And if those hash values match that proves integrity. Alice now knows that Bob has received exactly the same file that she sent and she knows Bob received it right because of the authenticity from the digital signature. So now that we have authenticity and integrity, that's where we now finally achieve non-repudiation of Bob cannot deny receiving this file because Alice says, look, I know you received it because you sent back, you know, contained within your digital signature that you sent back to me was this hash value. And the only way that you'd have this hash value is if you'd received this file. So that's non-repudiation of delivery. Bob can't deny that he received the file. There you go, that's the amazing Alice and Bob diagram. Something important I'll point out here, okay? There is no way that Alice and Bob are doing all this manually. Can you imagine at your company if you said, okay, now, if you put out a very simple 70-page memo saying, okay, from now on, everyone, when you want to send someone an email, I need you to just go ahead and open up the command prompt, pull up your private key, Go ahead and run the email that you want to send through the AES algorithm. Make sure you use a 256-bit key. And then hash that using, I don't know, a SHA-256. Take that hash value, encrypt it with your private... Can you imagine if users had to do all this manually? It would never work. So the only way that all this works is if Alice and Bob both have a crypto system installed on their computers. And the crypto system will take care of all of these steps automatically. A perfect example of a crypto system is a piece of software called PGP. It stands for Pretty Good Privacy. PGP is a free open source crypto system that anyone can install on their computer. So Alice and Bob could both install PGP. And one of the cool things about PGP is it even integrates with Outlook. So you can just go into Outlook and hit send on an email. And if you've configured PGP to do this, PGP will take care of encrypting the email. PGP will take care of generating the digital signature to attach the email. PGP will take care of all of that. And then as long as Bob has PGP on his system, it'll take care of decrypting the email and decrypting the digital signature and all this other stuff and sending back basically a read receipt. So I just wanted to highlight that. Alice and Bob are not doing this manually, right? It would not work. They need a crypto system. And those crypto systems have to be configured in the same way, right? Because if Alice encrypted with AES and then Bob tried to decrypt with triple DES, it wouldn't work. Or if Alice hashed with MD5 and then Bob hashed with SHA512, it wouldn't work. So their crypto systems also have to be configured the same way. And by the way, like when a message is sent, it will contain metadata as to which algorithms are used in what way. But the point is, they both need crypto systems. They both have to be configured in the same way. Okay, one final piece here. I told you at the beginning, if you're watching this video for the first time, don't copy anything down. Here's why. Pick up your pencil now, or this evening, or at some point. Try and redraw this diagram from memory. See if you can remember all the sort of major pieces here. So here's what I would suggest in writing down. Write down that Alice wants to send a large vial to Bob and achieve all five services, right? Confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, non-repudiation of both origin and delivery, and access control. Write that down and then see if you can redraw this Alice and Bob diagram and understand where you're using digital signatures versus digital certificates, where we're using symmetric versus asymmetric cryptography, et cetera. You will never have to draw a diagram like this on the exam. But if you can redraw this from memory and understand what's happening where, it's a really good test for yourself of do you understand the fundamentals of cryptography and how all these various pieces that we've talked about work together. So I would recommend that you do that. And if you get stuck, of course, you can come back and watch this video or look at the diagrams in our book or whatever. All the best in your drawing. Have fun with some crayons.
